# Timestamped Operating Log

This is the detailed live log for the Sinex conductor/devloop. Append entries
with wall-clock timestamps, elapsed-time awareness, and an explicit velocity
reflection. Keep it concrete enough that another harness can resume from this
file without chat context.

Use `.agent/scripts/devloop-log "short title"` to append a correctly-shaped
entry.

## Entry Template

```markdown
## 2026-06-30T03:12:46+02:00 — short title

Elapsed since previous entry: unknown | Xm
Loop phase: reflect
Primary aim: ...
Evidence touched: ...
Action taken: ...
Artifact/proof: ...
Velocity note: what got faster/slower, why, and what to change next.
Next decision: ...
```

## 2026-06-30T03:12:46+02:00 — time-aware scaffold added

Elapsed since previous entry: first entry in this log
Loop phase: orient
Primary aim: make the Sinex conductor setup track time and velocity explicitly.
Evidence touched: conductor prompt, current scratch index, current `.agent`
layout, prior RAM/IO cleanup findings.
Action taken: created this timestamped operating log, added a velocity/cadence
rubric, and added an append helper so future loops do not drift into vague
untimestamped notes.
Artifact/proof: `.agent/scratch/current/OPERATING-LOG.md`,
`.agent/scratch/current/VELOCITY.md`, `.agent/scripts/devloop-log`.
Velocity note: the previous setup had good content but weak temporal structure;
without timestamps it was hard to compute loop duration, detect stalls, or
notice acceleration opportunities. The new scaffold makes elapsed time and
acceleration part of every loop entry.
Next decision: on the next capability loop, choose one live-data demo slice and
record start time, verification time, artifact time, and friction removed.

Velocity note: process startup is now executable rather than purely mnemonic:
future loops can run status/review/sync and catch stale jobs, TODO logs, inbox
drift, and process pressure before broad work. Remaining drag: phase timing is
still markdown-only rather than structured JSONL, and demo manifest validation
is not automated yet.
Next decision: switch to Direction and choose the next live-data capability
slice from query/context/demo priorities.

## 2026-06-30T03:41:25+02:00 — ambient system recall tier under proof

Elapsed since previous entry: 0h 7m 19s
Loop phase: implement | verify
Focus: Evidence -> Construction -> Proof -> Velocity
Trigger: the current demo artifact showed `journald` as the dominant activity
family, proving the old self-observation split was insufficient for recall
salience because low-action system logs can still crowd the headline.
Primary aim: keep system log material visible while preventing it from defining
operator activity by raw event volume.
Evidence touched: `crate/sinex-primitives/src/views/events.rs`,
`crate/sinexctl/src/commands/context.rs`,
`crate/sinex-primitives/src/views/tests.rs`,
`/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/context-pack-current/context-pack.{md,json}`.
Action taken: added `ambient_system` and `ambient_system_total` to
`ContextRecallPackView`, routed material system-log families into that tier in
the CLI recall builder, rendered ambient system material between activity and
derived tiers, and updated fixtures/tests to prove `journald` no longer counts
as headline activity.
Artifact/proof: narrow xtask proof started for the shared markdown artifact and
sinexctl recall renderer tests; at 03:39 the first `xtask --bg` launcher was
still in pre-job bootstrap and had not registered in `xtask jobs`.
Velocity note: this is exactly the wait-time tactic case: the compile/test path
is useful but should not freeze the loop. Current drag is xtask pre-job
bootstrap/IO wait, not a Rust diagnostic yet; avoid duplicate heavy checks until
the launcher exits or is deliberately terminated.
Next decision: if the launcher returns, use the job ids/results; if it remains
stuck beyond the bounded poll, terminate the launcher and retry a narrower
foreground/list command only after checking IO pressure.

sinexd build latency plus IO pressure, not a reason to touch prod.
Next decision: once the dev gateway listens, regenerate

## 2026-06-30T04:16:28+02:00 — meta-audit

Loop phase: reflect | velocity
Focus: Meta -> Velocity
Trigger: operator corrected wait behavior and asked for proactive self-process improvement
Failure hypothesis: I was treating wait-time tactics as optional helpful work
rather than as a mandatory mode-rotation policy, and I was waiting for operator
correction before auditing process failures.
Evidence for/against: the loop did use wait time for useful work (Polylogue
scaffold inspection, docs, runtime-target implementation), but the triggers
were not explicit until the operator called it out. `devloop-wait` existed only
after that correction, and Meta mode did not exist at all.
Process/tooling change considered: make operator corrections and repeated
friction first-class Meta triggers, and require a concrete scaffold/tooling or
observability change when a process failure is real.
Change made now: added `devloop-meta`, added Meta as a focus mode, added
meta-audit triggers to PROCESS/RUNBOOK/VELOCITY, and updated `devloop-review`
to require the script.
Change deferred: no automated semantic check yet for "two polls without mode
rotation"; this may be added later if the pattern recurs.
Next tripwire: any future operator process correction or repeated friction must
produce either a filled meta-audit entry or a concrete scaffold/tooling change
before the next broad status claim.

## 2026-06-30T07:16:52+02:00 — DLQ preview classifier slice

Loop phase: implement | verify | artifact

## 2026-06-30T07:31:30+02:00 — self-observation timeout + xtask status repair

Loop phase: observe | implement | verify
Focus: Evidence -> Runtime lifecycle -> Tooling
Trigger: live DLQ tail grew from 208 to 219 after descriptor-prime restart, mostly grouped as `error_payload.slice_arrival_timeout`
Evidence: current tail timeout source materials are older self-observation IDs; post-restart self-observation descriptor materials still appeared as `sensing` with no bytes/blob, so descriptor append alone did not close the timeout path. NATS `DEV_SOURCE_MATERIAL` was empty with one consumer, meaning frames had been consumed and the DB material lifecycle was the remaining signal to fix.
Change in progress: `SelfObserver::prime` now writes the stable descriptor JSONL and immediately calls the shared buffered writer `flush("self-observation descriptor prime")`, finalizing the descriptor material while keeping the stream ready for later telemetry appends.
Tooling bug found during verification: `xtask infra status` classified active `xtask test -p sinexd`, `cargo`, and `rustc --crate-name sinexd` processes as dev-local `sinexd`, inflating runtime RSS/process counts. Root cause was cmdline matching against any argv segment named `sinexd`; fix in progress changes classification to require argv[0] basename `sinexd` and adds a regression test for xtask/rustc false positives.
Proof in progress: focused sinexd test job 2000408 is running; next proof is a focused xtask classifier test, then rebuilt runtime/live source-material proof.
Velocity note: this is the right kind of detour: the broken status surface directly obscured whether runtime or compile work owned memory, so fixing it improves the next loop rather than pausing object work.

## 2026-06-30T07:43:03+02:00 — self-observation descriptor finalize proof

Loop phase: verify | artifact | commit-prep
Focus: Proof -> Artifact -> Tooling
Changes: `SelfObserver::prime` now flushes after writing the stable descriptor JSONL, finalizing the descriptor material but keeping the buffered writer usable; `xtask infra status` now classifies dev-local sinexd by argv[0] basename instead of any cmdline segment.
Proof:
- `xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(self_observation_prime_descriptor_is_stable_jsonl) or test(buffered_append_writer_flushes_material_without_stopping)'` passed as job 2000408.
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(sinexd_process_classification_uses_executable_only)'` passed as job 2000410 after one import-fix rerun.
- `xtask build -p sinexd` passed as job 2000411.
- `git diff --check` passed.
Live runtime: restarted dev runtime as job 2000412; `xtask infra status --json` reports only PID 3928156 as sinexd with ~336 MB RSS, proving the process-classifier fix on the live surface.
Live source-material proof: `source-list-after-self-observation-prime-flush.json` shows descriptor-prime materials at 05:40:49Z completed with byte counts, while later telemetry materials can still be active `sensing` streams. Material show files prove selected completed descriptor rows have blob IDs and small byte counts: sinexd 119 bytes, health 141 bytes, analytics 145 bytes.
DLQ caveat: `dlq-list-after-self-observation-prime-flush.json` reached seq 231 and the tail groups as `error_payload.slice_arrival_timeout`, but those sampled IDs are older `019f16b3-*` one-hour timeouts, not the new `019f170b-*` descriptor materials.
Artifact updates: `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/README.md` and `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/README.md` updated with the precise proof/caveat.
Next decision: after committing, the next high-value slice is either paced DLQ cleanup/requeue for historical timeout fallout or reducing the test planner compile tax for exact-filter sinexd tests that still compile broad integration surfaces.

## 2026-06-30T07:49:50+02:00 — xtask textual-OR compile-tax repair

Loop phase: investigate | implement | verify
Focus: Velocity -> Tooling correctness
Trigger: job 2000408 spent 265s in `nextest-compile` for two inline sinexd unit tests because the command used textual `or`, and xtask's simple filter inference only understood symbolic `|`.
Finding: `xtask freshness explain` for a single `test(name)` sinexd filter already emitted `--lib`, but the original `test(a) or test(b)` proof key dropped `--lib` and added `--runtime-binary=sinexd:sinexd`.
Change: `extract_simple_test_name_terms` now accepts nextest textual `and`/`or` connectives while still rejecting negation, package predicates, and unsupported boolean words such as `xor`.
Proof:
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_extract_simple_test_name_terms_accepts_boolean_test_names) or test(test_textual_or_inline_unit_filters_infer_lib_target) or test(test_simple_test_name_term_count_rejects_complex_filters)'` passed as job 2000413.
- `xtask freshness explain test -- -p sinexd -E 'test(self_observation_prime_descriptor_is_stable_jsonl) or test(buffered_append_writer_flushes_material_without_stopping)' --json` now reports `--lib` and no `--runtime-binary`.
- Commit `4f95a2bef fix(xtask): infer lib targets for textual test filters`.
Velocity note: this is an actual acceleration improvement from the loop evidence: future two-inline-test sinexd proofs should avoid compiling broad integration surfaces and runtime binaries when the filter is exact and lib-only.

## 2026-06-30T08:36:34+02:00 — DLQ bounded cleanup + deleted-hole peek repair

Loop phase: implement | verify | artifact
Focus: Operator control -> Tooling brokenness -> Demo proof
Trigger: the live DLQ demo had enough attribution to justify cleanup, but a whole-stream purge would destroy useful historical pressure evidence.
Change: `DlqPurgeRequest` now accepts an inclusive `start_sequence`/`end_sequence` selector; `sinexctl ops dlq purge` validates complete positive ranges, formats table/JSON/YAML through the shared output registry, and logs range metadata in operation scope.
Broken step fixed instead of stepped over: after deleting newest sequences, `ops dlq peek --tail` hung because NATS `last_sequence` stayed above deleted holes. The handler now performs a finite direct sequence scan and skips `NotFound` stream holes.
Tooling repair: queued `xtask` results now report that no job id is available yet and give an explicit promotion command (`xtask jobs list --json`) instead of returning ambiguous success-shaped output.
Proof so far:
- `xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(dlq_purge_deletes_only_selected_sequence_range)'` passed as job 2000427.
- `xtask test -p sinexctl -E 'test(dlq_purge_selector_requires_complete_valid_range) or test(dlq_purge_table_formatter_renders_operation) or test(format_registry_exactly_covers_clap_leaf_commands)'` passed as job 2000425.
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_coordination_to_result_queued) or test(test_coordination_to_result_queued_pending_assignment)'` passed as job 2000418.
- Final deleted-hole regression proof passed:
  `xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(dlq_purge_deletes_only_selected_sequence_range) or test(dlq_peek_start_sequence_skips_deleted_stream_holes)'`
  completed as job 2000432.
Live artifact: `dlq-purge-sequence-250-259-clean-json-20260630T0629Z.json` proves a clean bounded purge of ten `slice_arrival_timeout` DLQ rows; before/after list and peek captures are recorded under `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/`.
Caveat: bounded cleanup is a control improvement, not the timeout root-cause fix. Remaining object work should still target current `slice_arrival_timeout` producers or a paced requeue/cleanup flow.
Velocity note: this slice improved both product control and development control. Job 2000432 spent ~58s in `runtime-binaries` and ~53s in `nextest-compile`; the actual test run took ~1.5s while peak job memory reached ~7.4 GiB. The remaining xtask issue is not just pre-job bootstrap opacity, but also avoiding runtime-binary preparation for handler tests when a narrower lib-style proof is possible.

## 2026-06-30T08:49:58+02:00 — final bounded-cleanup live proof

Loop phase: verify | artifact | commit-prep

- `xtask check --fmt -p sinex-primitives -p sinexd -p sinexctl -p xtask` passed as job 2000434 after one real formatter fix.
- `xtask check --fmt -p sinexd` passed as job 2000437 after removing the dead `require_stream_sequence` helper/test introduced by the finite-peek rewrite.
- `xtask build -p sinexd -p sinexctl` passed as job 2000435; final `xtask build -p sinexd` passed as job 2000438.

## 2026-06-30T08:55:50+02:00 — current self-observation timeout producer

Loop phase: observe | choose | implement
Focus: Live evidence -> Root cause -> Shared primitive use
Trigger: after bounded DLQ cleanup, fresh live `ops dlq list` still reported 244 messages, ~54.3 MB, and `pressure_level = critical`.
Evidence captured:
- `dlq-list-current-20260630T0652Z.json` and `dlq-peek-tail-20-current-20260630T0652Z.json`.
- Tail messages are all `error_payload.slice_arrival_timeout`; retained sequences are 246..249 and 260..264, with 250..259 intentionally deleted by the prior bounded purge.
- Source shows for the 9 current timeout material IDs confirm failed `sinex.self-observation.*` materials from the older pre-fix window: `optional_blob_id = null`, `total_bytes = null`, `event_count = 0`.
- `source-list-recent-200-20260630T0653Z.json` shows the current runtime still opens self-observation materials after descriptor completion. Example `source-show-current-sensing-selfobs-019f174a-a256-7573-a0c3-643e2a8f4a6e-20260630T0654Z.json` is `sinex.self-observation.analytics-automaton`, status `sensing`, `event_count = 1546`, but `optional_blob_id = null` and `total_bytes = null`.
Conclusion: descriptor priming fixed BEGIN-only descriptor materials, but normal self-observation telemetry batches can still remain open until the event-engine one-hour slice timeout. This is current-risk, not only historical fallout.
Change in progress: `SelfObserver::publish` now calls the shared `BufferedAppendStreamWriter::flush("self-observation telemetry batch")` immediately after materializing a telemetry record, so each materialized telemetry batch becomes a completed source material instead of a long-open append stream.
Regression proof: extended `self_observation_metrics_persist_via_event_engine` to assert the persisted event's source material is completed with bytes/blob and no `sensing` material remains for that self-observation component. `xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(self_observation_metrics_persist_via_event_engine)'` passed as job 2000440. Stage timing again shows compile/setup dominates: runtime-binaries ~59.7s, nextest-compile ~49.9s, actual test run ~3.9s, peak job memory ~7.3 GiB.
Velocity note: this keeps the fix in the existing acquisition/materialization primitive (`flush`) rather than adding another DLQ cleanup silo. The integration test is expensive, but it proves the actual runtime pipeline rather than only a local writer unit.

## 2026-06-30T09:04:40+02:00 — self-observation telemetry flush proof

Loop phase: verify | artifact | commit-prep
Focus: Runtime proof -> Demo update -> Tradeoff
Proof:
- `xtask check --fmt -p sinexd` failed first as job 2000441 on a real rustfmt issue, then passed as job 2000442 after formatting the new wait condition.
- `xtask build -p sinexd` passed as job 2000443.
- Dev-local runtime restarted from the rebuilt binary as job 2000444.
Live artifact:
- `runtime-ping` via the rebuilt target succeeded.
- `source-list-after-selfobs-telemetry-flush-20260630T0703Z.json` and `selfobs-material-summary-after-telemetry-flush-20260630T0703Z.tsv` show rapid completed `sinex.self-observation.*` materials with byte counts.
- `source-list-after-selfobs-telemetry-flush-20260630T0704Z.json` caught four newest materials in transient `sensing`, and `source-show-post-flush-recheck-*-20260630T0704Z.json` proves those exact IDs completed shortly afterward with blob IDs, byte counts, and event_count=1. Observed open window was under one second.
Conclusion: the self-observation telemetry path no longer leaves long-open append materials that can age into `slice_arrival_timeout`; the remaining DLQ rows are historical fallout unless fresh tails later show post-fix material IDs.
Caveat/tradeoff: per-batch flush raises self-observation material churn. It is the right correctness move for operator trust now, but the next perf/velocity slice should quantify churn and consider bounded-age flushing below the assembler timeout.

## 2026-06-30T09:10:06+02:00 — self-observation churn refinement

Loop phase: measure | reprioritize | implement
Focus: Correctness tradeoff -> Shared primitive
Trigger: the per-batch flush fixed timeout risk but the live DB summary showed 5,625 self-observation materials in the last 10 minutes, 5,624 completed, one current sensing, ~2.6 MB total, p50 431 bytes, p95 624 bytes, max open ~1.85s. High-volume components dominated: tag-applier 1208, entity-extractor 1192, document-parser 1187, instruction-expectation-reconciler 1173, analytics-automaton 1170.
Artifact: `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/selfobs-material-churn-db-summary-20260630T0706.tsv`.
Decision: keep the correctness property but remove one-material-per-event churn. Replace per-publish self-observation flush with a general `BufferedAppendStreamWriterConfig::max_open_duration` flush policy; configure self-observation for 5s max-open duration. The shared writer now flushes busy streams after age exceeds the bound and idle streams via a timer.
Proof: `xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(buffered_append_writer_flushes_after_max_open_duration) or test(self_observation_metrics_persist_via_event_engine)'` passed as job 2000445.
Verification cost: job 2000445 spent ~67.5s in runtime-binaries and ~220.4s in nextest-compile; nextest-run was ~13.5s. Peak job process memory was ~24.2 GiB, host memory ~25.4 GiB, host IO pressure some avg10 max ~85.8%, memory pressure some avg10 max ~24.3%. This is now strong evidence that exact integration-test verification can still explode compile/runtime-binary fanout and should become an xtask velocity/resource slice.
Final verification: `xtask check --fmt -p sinexd` passed as job 2000447 after one rustfmt fix; `xtask build -p sinexd` passed as job 2000448. Runtime restarted as job 2000449.
Live bounded-flush artifact:
- `selfobs-material-churn-db-summary-after-bounded-flush-from-restart-20260630T0722.tsv` measured only post-restart data: 67 self-observation materials, 61 completed, 6 current sensing, p50 14,124 bytes, p95 19,229 bytes, max open/completed duration ~6.7s.
- `selfobs-current-sensing-after-bounded-flush-recheck-20260630T0723.tsv` shows the remaining sensing rows are fresh current batches under ~2.4s old, not stale timeout candidates.
Conclusion: bounded shared writer flushing preserves the timeout fix while reducing the tiny-material churn by roughly an order of magnitude in the measured window.
Velocity note: this is a good example of not stopping at a correctness patch whose side effect is visible in live data; the loop used the artifact to immediately refine the substrate.

## 2026-06-30T09:36:18+02:00 — xtask overlap selector attribution repair

Loop phase: evidence-harness | tooling reliability | resource triage
Focus: Broken evidence path -> Operator/agent observability
Trigger: trying to analyze the expensive exact sinexd test run from job 2000445 with `xtask history overlap inv:2000445` silently resolved an unrelated old invocation #2000445 from 2026-06-06, producing null resource fields and a false-looking overlap report. The actual recent run was background job #2000445 mapped to invocation #2003258.
Change: `history overlap` now includes a `selector` block in JSON/human output. If an `inv:<id>` or bare numeric selector collides with a background job id that maps to a different invocation, it emits a warning telling the operator to use `job:<id>`. The command guide/reference now use `job:<id>` for IDs copied from `xtask jobs`.
Proof:
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_overlap_report_warns_when_inv_selector_collides_with_job_id)'` passed as job 2000450.
- `xtask check --fmt -p xtask` passed as job 2000452.
- `xtask docs check` passed after `xtask docs sync`.
Live evidence:
- `xtask history overlap job:2000445 --limit 3 --json` resolves to invocation #2003258 and shows the true expensive test resource shape: process memory max ~24.2 GiB, host memory max ~25.4 GiB, IO PSI some/full max 85.76%/67.95%, busiest device `sda`, and ~49.5 GiB busiest-device traffic. nix-daemon and nix-build slice memory stayed under ~289 MiB, so this run implicates the test process tree / compile-fanout path, not Nix daemon memory.
- `xtask history overlap inv:2000445 --limit 1 --json` still resolves the explicit invocation selector, but now warns that background job #2000445 maps to invocation #2003258 and says to use `job:2000445`.
Conclusion: the immediate observability bug was namespace confusion between job ids and invocation ids. Next resource slice should use `job:<id>` selectors consistently and target test-stage/fanout attribution for integration runs, especially around `runtime-binaries` and `nextest-compile`, rather than broad system limits.

## 2026-06-30T09:44:11+02:00 — stage breakdown promoted into overlap report

Loop phase: implement | verify | resource triage
Focus: One-command evidence projection
Trigger: after the selector fix, `history overlap job:2000445` showed whole-run pressure but still required a second `jobs status` call to see which stages consumed the time and pressure.
Change: `history overlap` JSON now includes recorded `StageTiming` rows, and human output prints a Stage Breakdown table with duration, success, and PSI avg10 snapshots.
Proof:
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_overlap_report_warns_when_inv_selector_collides_with_job_id)'` passed as job 2000453, now asserting the overlap JSON includes the recorded `nextest-compile` stage.
- `xtask check --fmt -p xtask` passed as job 2000455.
Live evidence: `xtask history overlap job:2000445 --limit 3 --json` now shows the expensive exact sinexd test run in one report: `nextest-compile` took 220.45s with IO full avg10 58.93 and ~87.86s IO-full stall; `runtime-binaries` took 67.53s with IO full avg10 20.73 and ~8.86s IO-full stall; `nextest-run` was only 13.50s. This makes the next target sharper: compile/runtime-binary preparation fanout and cache/bootstrap behavior, not test body time.
Velocity note: repeated `xtask` self-rebuilds before background job promotion added ~1-2 minutes of opaque foreground wait after each xtask source edit. That is now a concrete later xtask velocity issue: the wrapper bootstrap path needs progress/coordination visibility before the managed job exists.

## 2026-06-30T09:52:55+02:00 — wrapper rebuild substage attribution

Loop phase: evidence-harness | implement | verify
Focus: Pre-job xtask bootstrap opacity
Trigger: post-promotion `history overlap job:2000455` only explained the managed check job. `history wrapper-events --days 1 --limit 5` showed five recent wrapper rebuilds spent 453.5s wall, but only 129.9s was attributed to `xtask_build`; the rest of the foreground wait happened before any managed job existed.
Change: the devshell wrapper now records `sqlx_bootstrap` around the SQLx prep as well as sub-stages `sqlx_initdb`, `sqlx_postgres_start`, `sqlx_role_db`, `schema_apply_bootstrap_bin`, and `schema_apply_bootstrap` when those steps run. It also emits a progress line before the cargo build stage. Existing wrapper-event parsing/reporting already handles arbitrary stage keys, so this collapses the hidden wrapper work into the existing history wrapper-events projection.
Proof:
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_read_wrapper_events_filters_and_skips_malformed_lines) or test(test_wrapper_stage_totals_rank_by_duration)'` passed as job 2000456.
- Fresh-wrapper verification via `nix develop --command bash -lc 'SINEX_XTASK_FORCE_REBUILD=1 xtask history wrapper-events --days 1 --limit 1 --json'` passed and produced an event with `sqlx_bootstrap`, `sqlx_role_db`, `schema_apply_bootstrap_bin`, `schema_apply_bootstrap`, and `xtask_build`.
- `nix flake check --no-build` passed.
- `xtask check --fmt -p xtask` passed as job 2000457.
- `xtask docs check` passed.
Live evidence: the forced fresh-wrapper rebuild at 2026-06-30T07:51Z took 50.377s total. New stage attribution shows warm SQLx/bootstrap was not the culprit in that run: `sqlx_bootstrap` 0.629s, `schema_apply_bootstrap_bin` 0.175s, `schema_apply_bootstrap` 0.371s, `sqlx_role_db` 0.018s, and `xtask_build` 49.239s. This sharply redirects the next velocity target from generic wrapper bootstrap to the cargo/xtask self-build path and/or avoiding rebuilds when only non-xtask inputs changed.
Residual: older wrapper events remain partially attributed because they were recorded before this instrumentation. Future rebuilds should show the missing wall-time components.

## 2026-06-30T10:01:25+02:00 — xtask wrapper rebuild trigger and stale-lock repair

Loop phase: evidence-harness | fix broken step | verify
Focus: Wrapper reliability + pre-job velocity
Trigger: the wrapper history exposed a concrete bad rebuild trigger: a `sources_newer` event at 2026-06-30T07:48Z took 75.232s and was triggered only by `flake.nix` being newer than the checkout-local xtask binary. A Cargo-built `target/debug/xtask` cannot incorporate devshell/wrapper changes from `flake.nix`; re-entering `nix develop` is the mechanism for those changes. Separately, `xtask docs check` hung in a wrapper sleep loop because `.sinex/state/xtask-build.lock` contained only `stages.json` and no `pid` file, so the old wait loop had no way to classify or remove the stale lock.
Change: removed `flake.nix` from the wrapper's Cargo-built xtask freshness inputs and trigger reporting, with comments clarifying binary inputs vs devshell inputs. Added stale-lock recovery for build locks whose pid is dead or whose pid file is missing after a short grace period, with explicit warnings before removal.
Proof:
- `nix develop --command bash -lc 'xtask check --fmt -p xtask --bg'` started job 2000458 without printing a checkout-local xtask rebuild line; `xtask jobs wait 2000458` passed.
- `xtask history wrapper-events --days 1 --limit 2 --json` after that run showed no new flake-only rebuild event; the latest events were still the earlier forced rebuild and the pre-fix flake-only event.
- `nix flake check --no-build` passed.
- `nix develop --command bash -lc 'xtask docs check'` passed and reported all generated surfaces up to date.
- Forced stale-lock proof: with the pidless stale lock still present, `nix develop --command bash -lc 'SINEX_XTASK_FORCE_REBUILD=1 xtask --version'` printed `Removing stale xtask build lock with no pid file`, rebuilt, and returned `xtask 0.1.0`.
Live evidence: the new forced rebuild event at 2026-06-30T08:01Z took 7.187s total; top stage was `sqlx_bootstrap` 4.257s, with `xtask_build` only 2.301s. This validates both the stale-lock fix and the wrapper-event stage attribution from the previous slice.
Conclusion: this fixes an actual broken xtask step rather than stepping around it. The next velocity target should keep using wrapper-events plus overlap stages to distinguish avoidable rebuild triggers from real compile/test fanout.

## 2026-06-30T10:25:35+02:00 — wrapper rebuilds composed into overlap evidence

Loop phase: implement | verify | dogfood artifact
Focus: Collapse evidence silo -> One-command latency/resource projection
Trigger: after wrapper-event instrumentation, user-perceived xtask latency was still split across `history wrapper-events` for pre-job rebuilds and `history overlap` for managed invocation stages/resources. That forced agents to mentally join two reports and made pre-job rebuilds easy to miss.
Change: `xtask history overlap` now includes a `wrapper_events` section in JSON and human output. It uses the shared wrapper JSONL parser directly, preserving read-only DB behavior, and falls back to already-persisted `wrapper_events` rows when the JSONL has no matching rows. Matching is explicit time association: wrapper events that overlap the invocation or finish within five minutes before it are shown. The report also gained a legacy timestamp parser fallback for SQLite-style history strings like `2026-06-30 8:07:19.905335656 +00:00:00`, which the new tests exposed as a real latent parser bug.
Proof:
- `SINEX_XTASK_FORCE_REBUILD=1 xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_parse_history_time_accepts_legacy_sqlite_offset_shape) or test(test_overlap_report_warns_when_inv_selector_collides_with_job_id)' --bg` passed as job 2000465 after earlier failed attempts exposed and fixed the readonly-DB write mistake and timestamp parser bug.
- `xtask check --fmt -p xtask` passed as job 2000466.
- `xtask docs sync && xtask docs check` passed after regenerating `xtask/docs/command-guide.md` and the generated agent surface.
Live artifact:
- `xtask history overlap job:2000465 --limit 8 --json` now returns `wrapper_events` alongside `stages` and `shared_resources`. The report showed the managed test invocation took 29.905s, with `nextest-compile` 26.733s and host `io_full_avg10` max 15.74. It also showed three nearby wrapper rebuild events, including the immediate forced rebuild overlapping the invocation by 4.19s (`xtask_build` 1.957s) and the prior forced rebuild finishing 1.81s before target start (`xtask_build` 24.463s).
Conclusion: one overlap report now reconstructs both pre-job wrapper rebuild cost and managed job stage/resource cost. The next velocity target should reduce repeated forced/full xtask rebuild churn, but the evidence path is no longer split across separate commands.
Residual: `xtask fix --fmt-only` also formatted three unrelated Rust files. They are formatter-only changes and should be committed separately as style or left for the owning slice, not mixed into this xtask evidence commit.

## 2026-06-30T10:47:09+02:00 — xtask status liveness false-negative repair

Loop phase: fix broken step | dogfood live runtime | verify
Focus: Runtime/status observability correctness
Trigger: after restarting dev-local `sinexd` as job 2000467, `xtask status --summary` reported `event_engine_status: down` and `Runtime health: event_engine is down` even while the daemon logs showed active event-engine work and fresh telemetry. Direct DB inspection showed the root cause: `core.runs` had no live `sinexd` heartbeat row, but `core.events` had fresh `event_engine.consumer.lag.pending` gauge samples within ~5-30s. The status path treated absence of the heartbeat row as authoritative and ignored fresh event-engine telemetry as liveness evidence.
Change: `RuntimeMetrics` now derives event-engine liveness from the union of heartbeat evidence and fresh event-engine telemetry. The runtime snapshot signal was renamed from `event_engine_heartbeat` to `event_engine_liveness`, and its message says which evidence source proved liveness (for example, `consumer lag telemetry 28s ago`). In the same status surface, an intentionally skipped gateway readiness probe owned by `sinexctl`/NixOS is now reported as `skipped` rather than `unknown`, so `xtask status` no longer manufactures `Core services not healthy: sinexd` when a live background `sinexd` exists.
Proof:
- `xtask check --fmt -p xtask --bg` passed as job 2000476.
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_event_engine_status_accepts_fresh_telemetry_without_heartbeat) or test(test_event_engine_status_marks_stale_telemetry_as_stale) or test(test_runtime_assessment_marks_degraded_on_stale_signals) or test(test_gateway_service_status_keeps_owned_skip_non_failure_for_live_process) or test(test_gateway_service_status_maps_ready_probe_skip_to_skipped) or test(test_runtime_snapshot_reports_event_engine_liveness_from_fresh_telemetry)' --bg` reused fresh successful proof from invocation 2003302.
- `git diff --check` passed.
Live artifact: `xtask status --summary` against the running dev daemon now reports `event_engine_status: healthy`, `consumer_lag_pending: 0.0`, `last_heartbeat_age_secs: null`, and runtime snapshot signal `event_engine_liveness` with message `consumer lag telemetry 28s ago`. Gateway readiness is `skipped` with the ownership explanation; the only remaining status warnings are uncommitted changes and the pre-existing drift-guard bypass history.
Conclusion: this fixes the broken xtask version/status step rather than stepping over it. The remaining observability opportunity is upstream: either make `core.runs` heartbeat coverage truthful for unified `sinexd`, or explicitly retire that heartbeat as primary liveness evidence in favor of event-engine-owned telemetry.

## 2026-06-30T10:52:59+02:00 — wrapper rebuild stage-gap instrumentation

Loop phase: evidence-harness | wrapper velocity | fix broken observability
Focus: Pre-job xtask rebuild attribution
Trigger: after composing wrapper events into `history overlap`, recent `sources_newer` rebuilds still showed a large blind spot: four events consumed 358.7s wall, but only 105.1s was attributed to `xtask_build`. Direct depfile measurement ruled out trigger input scanning as the culprit (453 depfile inputs parsed in milliseconds), so the issue was insufficient wrapper stage coverage around the foreground rebuild path.
Change: the devshell wrapper now records explicit stages for `rebuild_trigger_capture`, `sqlx_postgres_probe`, and `sqlx_postgres_stop`, in addition to the existing SQLx/schema/cargo stages. The change is intentionally small in `flake.nix` and avoids accepting unrelated formatter churn in the large embedded shell block.
Proof:
- `nix eval .#devShells.x86_64-linux.default.inputDerivation --raw >/dev/null` passed.
- `nix develop --command bash -lc 'SINEX_XTASK_FORCE_REBUILD=1 xtask --version >/tmp/sinex-xtask-version-proof.txt && xtask history wrapper-events --days 1 --limit 1 --json'` passed and exercised the fresh devshell wrapper.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- `xtask status --summary` remained healthy for the live dev daemon: `event_engine:ok lag:0`, gateway readiness `skipped`, only dirty-tree and drift-guard-history warnings.
Live artifact: the forced rebuild at `2026-06-30T08:52:07Z` took 24.551s and now reports stages: `xtask_build` 20.564s, `sqlx_bootstrap` 3.407s, `schema_apply_bootstrap_bin` 2.905s, `rebuild_trigger_capture` 0.499s, `schema_apply_bootstrap` 0.400s, `sqlx_role_db` 0.049s, `sqlx_postgres_probe` 0.020s, and `sqlx_postgres_stop` 0.001s.
Conclusion: future wrapper events should no longer hide trigger/probe/stop time. The earlier 80-94s `sources_newer` events remain historical evidence with missing attribution; the next fresh `sources_newer` event should be used to decide whether the remaining excess is actually cargo build variance, Nix schema-bootstrap resolution, or lock/wait behavior outside the recorded rebuild event.
Resource note: while verifying, `free -h` showed RAM usable (about 14GiB available) but swap nearly full (3.9GiB of 4.0GiB). That is not explained by this wrapper slice and remains a resource-state thread to inspect with process/cgroup attribution rather than guessing from aggregate memory alone.

## 2026-06-30T11:05:20+02:00 — Sinnix earlyoom swap-overflow policy repaired

Loop phase: host reliability | evidence-first resource fix | live verification
Focus: Prevent full swap from becoming hidden working memory while preserving agent/dev control-plane surfaces
Trigger: live host state showed about 15-17GiB MemAvailable but the 4GiB swapfile was ~3.9GiB used. The running earlyoom command was `-m15 -s25`; earlyoom requires BOTH thresholds to be crossed, so high MemAvailable meant nearly-full swap did not trigger any action. Process attribution showed the largest useful swapped process was `rust-analyzer` (~864MiB swap, ~1.6GiB RSS), with smaller swap spread across kitty/Chrome/MCP/Serena/Codex surfaces.
Change:
- Sinnix commit `e025f69 fix(memory): make swap pressure trip earlyoom` raised the memory threshold to 95%, kept the initial swap threshold at 25%, preferred Rust tooling including rust-analyzer, and avoided agent/MCP Python surfaces.
- Live activation proved the first policy was too blunt: after killing rust-analyzer and recovering swap to about 25%, earlyoom kept selecting unrelated processes (`nix`, `nix-daemon`, `codebase-memory`, checkout-local `sinexd`). That was not acceptable and directly matched the user's warning not to step over brokenness.
- Sinnix commit `d85f54a fix(memory): avoid killing control-plane processes` corrected the policy to `-m95 -s20`, removed Nix from preferred victims, and explicitly avoids `codebase-memory`, `sinexd`, `nix`, and `nix-daemon`.
Proof:
- `nix fmt -- --fail-on-change modules/performance.nix` passed for both Sinnix changes.
- Nix evaluation showed `freeMemThreshold = 95`, then corrected `freeSwapThreshold = 20`, with `extraArgs` preferring only `(cargo|rustc|rust-analyzer|cc1plus|ld)` and avoiding the agent/MCP/dev-control surfaces.
- `nix develop --command switch` completed after the correction and installed `/nix/store/3c24sshyb67izqf8kc296qp8xm2yrk4p-nixos-system-sinnix-prime-26.11.20260616.567a49d`.
- Live earlyoom after the corrected switch runs as `earlyoom -m95 -n -r3600 -s20 --prefer "(cargo|rustc|rust-analyzer|cc1plus|ld)" --avoid "...|codebase-memory|sinexd|nix|nix-daemon"`.
- Post-correction earlyoom journal from 11:04 onward shows startup/config only, no further kills; swap sits around 3.0GiB used / 1.0GiB free, above the 20% trip point.
- Restarted checkout-local Sinex core with `xtask run core --bg`; new `sinexd` PID `564778`, job `2000477`. `xtask status --summary` reports infra ok, background `sinexd` healthy, event-engine liveness healthy from telemetry (`consumer lag telemetry 18s ago`, lag 8).
Conclusion: the real bug was not "RAM is used" but that the configured 4GiB file swap was acting like silent working memory because earlyoom's memory threshold prevented the swap threshold from mattering. The final policy makes swap occupancy actionable without making Nix activation, MCP context, or dev-local daemons preferred victims. Further work should still improve cgroup/oom_score policy for agent scopes and expose top swap/PSI attribution in a first-class Sinnix/Lynchpin report.

## 2026-06-30T11:22:05+02:00 — live pressure report gains swap/process attribution

Loop phase: dogfood artifact | fix broken observability | verify on live host
Focus: Promote the resource incident's ad hoc shell probes into reusable xtask evidence
Trigger: the earlyoom/swap incident required manual `/proc/*/status`, cgroup, `free`, `swapon`, PSI, and journal probes to answer "what is actually occupying swap and what is causing pressure?" Existing `xtask analytics pressure` exposed PSI and optional top IO, but did not attribute current swap residency. In parallel, fresh wrapper events showed another honesty gap: a `sources_newer` rebuild took 85.313s but only 27.479s was attributed to `xtask_build`; a follow-up rebuild took 73.305s but only 16.927s was attributed.
Change:
- Added `xtask analytics pressure --top-swap`, returning PID, swap KiB, RSS KiB, cgroup, and command from `/proc/PID/status`, `/proc/PID/cgroup`, and command-line/comm.
- JSON output now includes `top_swap`; human output renders a "Top swap occupants" table.
- Updated the pressure recommendation to point at `xtask analytics pressure --top-swap --top-io`.
- Added command-guide/reference docs for `xtask analytics pressure`.
- Made wrapper rebuild event recording add `wrapper_unattributed` when named stages do not sum close to wrapper wall time, so future reports preserve unexplained foreground latency instead of silently dropping it.
Proof:
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_pressure_recommendation_classifies_measured_bands) or test(test_parse_proc_status_memory_reads_rss_and_swap) or test(test_parse_proc_status_memory_defaults_missing_fields_to_zero) or test(test_format_kib_uses_operator_scale_units)' --bg` passed as job 2000483.
- `xtask check --fmt -p xtask --bg` passed as job 2000482.
- `xtask docs sync` regenerated the command docs and `AGENTS.md`; `xtask docs check` passed afterward.
- `nix eval .#devShells.x86_64-linux.default.inputDerivation --raw >/dev/null` passed for the wrapper change.
- Synthetic jq proof for wrapper gaps produced `{"xtask_build":1000,"sqlx_bootstrap":500,"wrapper_unattributed":1500}` from a 3000ms total and 1500ms named stages.
- `git diff --check` passed.
Live artifact:
- `xtask analytics pressure --top-swap --top-io --sample-ms 1500 --limit 6 --json` reported `level=elevated`, `io.full avg10 24.91%`, `memory.full avg10 2.85%`, and `cpu.some avg10 0.24%`.
- Top swap occupants were kitty (~550MiB swap), Chrome renderer (~257MiB), Lynchpin MCP Python (~244MiB), Serena (~171MiB), another Chrome process (~148MiB), and noctalia (~148MiB). This immediately confirms the post-earlyoom state no longer has rust-analyzer as the dominant swap resident.
- Top IO over the 1.5s window was checkout-local `sinexd`, Codex processes, NATS, and Postgres background/connection processes. This does not prove causal ownership for the high PSI window, but it gives the next probe an inspectable starting point.
Conclusion: this turns the last resource incident into a reusable Sinex devloop demo surface: an agent can now produce an inspectable live pressure/swap-owner report from xtask instead of reconstructing it with bespoke shell loops. Next resource slice should either join this live view with Lynchpin/Sinnix historical pressure episodes or reduce the unexplained wrapper foreground gap now preserved as `wrapper_unattributed`.

## 2026-06-30T11:38:47+02:00 — top-IO attribution and wrapper stage honesty

Loop phase: fix broken xtask version | live observability | verify while runtime active
Focus: Make resource and wrapper evidence compositional instead of split by report surface
Trigger: after adding `--top-swap`, the live `--top-io` rows still lacked the same process context, so IO and swap attribution were two silos. A fresh forced wrapper rebuild also exposed a second broken step: `xtask history wrapper-events` summed nested stage durations as if they were exclusive wall time, making `sqlx_bootstrap` and `schema_apply_bootstrap_bin` percentages add up misleadingly.
Change:
- `xtask analytics pressure --top-io` now attaches command, cgroup, RSS, and swap KiB to every sampled IO-delta row using the same `/proc` process context helper as `--top-swap`.
- `/proc/PID/cgroup` parsing is factored/tested so unified cgroup paths are stable in JSON and human tables.
- Wrapper event stage totals now label each stage as `top_level` or `nested`; only top-level/exclusive phases get `% WALL`. Nested phases remain visible as detail but no longer pretend to consume additional wall time.
- Wrapper top-stage selection, including the overlap report, now prefers top-level stages and falls back to all stages only when no known top-level stage exists.
Proof so far:
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_parse_proc_status_memory_reads_rss_and_swap) or test(test_parse_proc_status_memory_defaults_missing_fields_to_zero) or test(test_format_kib_uses_operator_scale_units) or test(test_parse_proc_cgroup_returns_unified_cgroup_path)' --bg` passed as job 2000485.
- `xtask check --fmt -p xtask --bg` passed as job 2000486 before the wrapper report follow-up.
- `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_read_wrapper_events_filters_and_skips_malformed_lines) or test(test_wrapper_stage_totals_rank_by_duration) or test(test_parse_proc_cgroup_returns_unified_cgroup_path)' --bg` passed as job 2000488.
- `xtask check --fmt -p xtask --bg` passed as job 2000489 after the wrapper report follow-up.
- `git diff --check` passed after the wrapper edits.
Live artifact:
- `xtask analytics pressure --top-io --sample-ms 1000 --limit 5 --json` now returns `cgroup`, `rss_kib`, and `swap_kib` for each IO row. In the final proof window, top rows included Codex (`rss_kib=311464`, `swap_kib=127628`), checkout-local `sinexd` (`rss_kib=293820`, `swap_kib=0`), NATS, another Codex process, and a Postgres connection, all with cgroup paths.
- `xtask history wrapper-events --days 1 --limit 8 --json` now reports `schema_apply_bootstrap_bin` 54.013s and `schema_apply_bootstrap` 0.330s as `scope=nested` with `pct_of_total=null`, while top-level stages such as `xtask_build` 187.605s and `sqlx_bootstrap` 54.388s retain wall percentages. The specific 91.029s forced rebuild at `2026-06-30T09:29:03Z` now has top stage `sqlx_bootstrap` rather than the nested `schema_apply_bootstrap_bin`.
Conclusion: this is the immediate "do not step over broken xtask" repair. Live resource rows now carry enough cgroup/memory context to decide whether a writer is an agent, daemon, browser, or build process, and wrapper reports no longer inflate wall attribution by double-counting nested stages.

## 2026-06-30T11:54:00+02:00 — DLQ material-corruption preview repair in progress

Loop phase: dogfood evidence | broken-step repair | root-cause triage
Focus: Make the current live DLQ corruption bucket explain itself through the supported `sinexctl ops dlq peek` surface.
Trigger: live DLQ tail advanced to seq 294..298 with `material assembly corruption detected`; raw NATS payloads showed the useful explanation lived under nested `context.reason`, but the current preview path could truncate bulky nested context before exposing the reason and expected/actual counters.
Evidence:
- Current `ops dlq list` reports `total_messages=278`, `total_bytes=54316675`, `first_seq=1`, `last_seq=298`, `pressure_level=critical`.
- Raw payloads captured in `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/` show all five corruption rows are self-observation materials with exactly one extra staged slice relative to the END totals. Example seq 294: `assembled_bytes=1825`, `expected_bytes=1284`, `slice_count=4`, `expected_slices=3`, reason `assembled_bytes=1825 exceeds expected_bytes=1284`.
- The retained `DEV_SOURCE_MATERIAL` stream is workqueue-retained and no longer contains the historical frames, so frame ordering for these exact failures cannot be reconstructed directly.
Change in progress:
- `payload_preview` now recursively prioritizes nested structured objects instead of serializing bulky nested JSON in arbitrary order.
- Preview key priority now lifts `reason`, `expected_bytes`, `assembled_bytes`, `expected_slices`, and `slice_count` before bulky fields and lower-signal timestamps.
- Added a focused regression fixture from the live seq 294 shape: `payload_preview_keeps_nested_material_corruption_reason`.
Broken-step handling:
- First focused run, job 2000490, failed because `expected_bytes` still landed beyond a 260-character cutoff. That was not ignored; priority ordering was tightened so the expected/actual counters are in the front of nested context.
- Rerun job 2000491 passed:
  `xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(payload_preview_keeps_structured_error_before_bulky_context) or test(payload_preview_keeps_nested_material_corruption_reason)'`.
- Formatter/package check job 2000492 passed:
  `xtask check --fmt -p sinexd`.
Artifact:
- Updated `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/README.md` with a material-corruption preview follow-up and linked diagnosis note.
Current root-cause read:
- `AppendStreamAcquirer::finalize` takes and clears the active handle, so explicit buffered-writer finalization should rotate before later appends.
- The remaining suspicious zones are source-material frame identity/redelivery/order and assembler slice-after-END handling. `handle_slice` snapshots `pending_end.is_some()` before deferred slice I/O and dispatches finalize after applying the slice; that is valid for missing-slice completion, but a focused reproduction should pin what happens for an accepted extra slice after an END total.

## 2026-06-30T12:00:20+02:00 — assembler slice-after-END claim fix under test

Loop phase: object-level fix | evidence-backed race repair
Focus: Prevent a completed pending-END material from accepting a same-material extra slice before the decoupled finalization worker claims the state.
Reasoning:
- The raw DLQ corruption pattern is exactly `assembled_bytes > expected_bytes` and `slice_count = expected_slices + 1`.
- `handle_slice` can complete a material whose END arrived earlier, then dispatch finalization asynchronously.
- Before this change, the state remained `Accumulating` until the worker acquired the material lock and set `Finalizing`, so an extra/duplicate same-material slice could be accepted in that gap and mutate staged bytes past the END totals.
Change in progress:
- Added `PendingEndBehavior::Claimed` and a `dispatch_claimed_finalize` path so a caller that already marked the state `Finalizing` can still run the finalization worker as owner.
- `handle_slice` now re-locks after deferred I/O and claims finalization only if the pending END is complete: no buffered gaps, `expected_offset == end.total_size_bytes`, and `slice_count >= end.total_slices`.
- Added deterministic regression `slice_completed_pending_end_claims_finalization_before_extra_slice`: it holds the finalize semaphore, completes an out-of-order END via slice, verifies state is `Finalizing`, sends an extra slice, and checks the extra slice did not advance `expected_offset` or `slice_count`.
Verification:
- `git diff --check` passed before launching tests.
- Job 2000493 failed in compile due to test mistakes (`source_identifier` needed `String`, `SourceMaterialRecord` field is `total_bytes` not `total_size_bytes`). Fixed directly.
- Job 2000494 passed:
  `xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(slice_completed_pending_end_claims_finalization_before_extra_slice) or test(handle_end_before_slice_waits_for_missing_slice_instead_of_failing)'`.
  Both test bodies ran in under one second; wall time was compile/setup dominated.
- Job 2000495 failed at fmt; applied the rustfmt diff directly.
- Job 2000496 passed:
  `xtask check --fmt -p sinexd`.
- Job 2000497 passed:
  `xtask build -p sinexd`.
- Started rebuilt checkout-local runtime as job 2000498 with `xtask run core --bg`.
- `sinexctl --runtime-target .sinex/state/runtime-target.json runtime gateway ping --format json`
  returned `{"response":"pong"}`.
- `xtask status --summary` reported background `sinexd` running and event-engine liveness healthy (`consumer lag telemetry 18s ago`, `8 pending`).
- Live DLQ after restart did not grow: `total_messages=278`, `last_seq=298`.
- Captured live artifacts:
  `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/dlq-list-after-assembler-claim-fix-20260630T101408Z.json`
  and
  `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/dlq-peek-tail-after-assembler-claim-fix-20260630T101408Z.json`.
Tooling note:
- `xtask history diagnostics --invocation job:2000493 --level error --json` rejected `--invocation`; job stderr logs supplied the needed compiler diagnostics. This is a minor xtask ergonomics gap to fix later if diagnostics-by-invocation is intended.
- During job 2000494, `xtask jobs status` reported `status=running` for a short window after the process tree had exited and `junit.xml` existed, then caught up to success. Worth watching as a status freshness/reaper latency issue, but it resolved without manual cleanup.

## 2026-06-30T13:11:50+02:00 — DLQ cleanup plan distinguishes safe residue from changing tail

Loop phase: cleanup safety | operator projection | live evidence
Focus: Turn the enriched triage view into a non-destructive cleanup plan, so
operators and agents can tell which sampled DLQ ranges are bounded historical
residue and which still need investigation.

Code changes:
- Added `sinexctl ops dlq cleanup-plan --tail N`.
- The command reuses `dlq.list`, tail `dlq.peek`, and `sources.show` through the
  triage projection, then classifies each sampled group as `purge_candidate` or
  `inspect_only`.
- A group is a purge candidate only when it is contiguous in the retained
  sequence range, has material IDs, has complete material lookup coverage, and
  every material lookup is found with terminal `failed` status and a recorded
  failure reason.
- Interleaved/non-contiguous groups, missing material IDs, lookup failures, or
  non-terminal material state are blocked with explicit reasons.
- Registered the command in command-path metadata, output-format capabilities,
  backing RPC methods, and completion as read-only/non-destructive.
- Extended the devshell xtask launcher again: no-compile commands may use the
  existing checkout-local xtask binary while sources are newer, avoiding a
  rebuild/schema apply merely to run fmt.

## 2026-06-30T13:18:10+02:00 — Cleanup plan now splits reason buckets into contiguous action ranges

Loop phase: action model refinement | live evidence | runtime recovery
Focus: Avoid a false binary between “whole reason bucket is safe” and “whole
reason bucket is blocked.” Cleanup actions are sequence-range actions, so the
projection should split a reason bucket into contiguous retained ranges before
classifying safety.

Evidence:

  `299..303` as five contiguous `slice_arrival_timeout` rows.

  five self-observation modules whose materials started around
  `2026-06-30T10:04:55Z` and failed at `2026-06-30T11:05:36Z` after the
  one-hour timeout.
- `selfobs-timeout-followup-db-summary-20260630T111350.tsv` shows the same
  modules completed 93-94 blob-backed materials each after `11:05:36Z`, with
  only one current sensing row each. That makes the new timeout rows delayed
  historical finalization, not proof of continuing source failure.

Code changes:
- `dlq_triage_report` now splits each reason/original-subject bucket into
  contiguous message runs before emitting `DlqTriageGroup`s.
- `cleanup-plan` therefore classifies action ranges, not monolithic reason
  buckets. Interleaved reason buckets no longer block unrelated contiguous
  subranges.
- Unit expectations were updated: the earlier synthetic interleaved group now
  gets an exact single-sequence purge command, while non-terminal material
  status remains a blocker.

records and legitimately do not carry material IDs. Requiring source-material
evidence for that reason bucket blocked 193 rows that should be eligible for a
bounded operator purge plan.

- `dlq_cleanup_plan_item` now requires material lookup/status evidence only for
  reason buckets where material evidence is meaningful.

  strings: `reason_contract=duplicate_occurrence_suppression` and
  `material_lookup=not_required_for_this_reason`.
- Material-backed failures keep the previous safety contract: material IDs must
  be extracted, lookup coverage must match, materials must be found/failed, and
  failure reasons must exist.
- Added a focused regression test proving duplicate buckets with no material IDs
  become purge candidates and emit a bounded purge command.

## 2026-06-30T13:41:45+02:00 — Wide sanitized DLQ previews close the cleanup decision surface

Loop phase: projection generalization | live evidence | demo artifact
Focus: The two remaining cleanup blockers were not intrinsically unsafe; the
operator projection did not expose enough structured failure context to classify
them. The old 200-character `dlq.peek` preview showed only
`material_persist_failed`, while raw NATS payload evidence showed duplicate blob
upsert failures against already completed source materials.

Code changes:
- `DlqPeekRequest` now carries `payload_preview_chars` with a compact default.
  The gateway still applies disclosure policy before truncation and clamps the
  requested preview size to a bounded range.
- `sinexctl ops dlq peek` exposes `--payload-preview-chars`; MCP
  `sinex.dlq_peek` exposes the same field plus the previously missing
  `start_sequence` schema.
- `triage` and `cleanup-plan` request wider bounded sanitized previews so
  evidence projections can classify nested structured failure contexts without
  forcing a separate raw-NATS path.
- `cleanup-plan` now recognizes `material_persist_failed` rows as
  `completed_duplicate_blob_upsert` candidates only when every referenced
  material is found, completed, blob-backed, has persisted events, lacks a
  failure reason, and the bounded preview contains the duplicate blob constraint
  evidence for that material.

Verification:

  passed after the final clamp edit.

## 2026-06-30T13:53:20+02:00 — Cleanup plan now separates evidence items from coalesced actions

Loop phase: action algebra | live evidence | demo artifact
Focus: Once the retained DLQ was fully classified, the next bottleneck was
operator ergonomics: 13 per-reason candidate groups meant 13 potential purge
commands even though the purge endpoint can safely iterate sequence ranges and
skip missing stream gaps. The projection needed to preserve per-reason evidence
while offering fewer bounded action ranges.

Code changes:
- `DlqCleanupPlan` now includes `coalesced_actions` derived from candidate
  items.
- Coalescing merges adjacent or overlapping purge-candidate sequence ranges,
  preserves total message/group counts, and records the reason buckets covered
  by each action.
- Detailed `items` remain unchanged as the evidence/audit surface.
- The table renderer prints `Coalesced purge actions` before detailed items.

## 2026-06-30T14:06:15+02:00 — DLQ completion now exposes safe scopes and destructive flags

Loop phase: discoverability | agent ergonomics | demo artifact
Focus: `--all-retained` existed as a direct command, but structured completion
still returned no candidates for `sinexctl ops dlq cleanup-plan --`. That made
the capability less likely to be used by agents and humans in the loop.

Code changes:
- `_complete` now detects DLQ subcommand context and returns option candidates
  instead of only top-level DLQ actions.
- `cleanup-plan --` and `triage --` suggest `--all-retained` and `--tail` as
  read-only options.
- `purge --` suggests sequence/confirm flags with `danger=destructive`; requeue
  flags remain `danger=write`.

Verification:
- `nix develop --command xtask check --fmt -p sinexctl` passed.
- Job 2000541 passed:
  `xtask test -p sinexctl -E 'test(ops_dlq_completion_suggests_all_retained_for_cleanup_plan) or test(ops_dlq_completion_suggests_all_retained_for_triage) or test(ops_dlq_completion_marks_purge_options_destructive) or test(ops_dlq_completion_suggests_cleanup_plan_as_read_only)'`.
- Job 2000542 passed: `xtask build -p sinexctl`.

Live artifact:
- Captured `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/dlq-completion-cleanup-plan-options-20260630T120603Z.json`,
  `dlq-completion-triage-options-20260630T120603Z.json`, and
  `dlq-completion-purge-options-20260630T120603Z.json`.
- Completion now returns `--all-retained` with `danger=none` for read-only
  projection commands and keeps purge options marked `danger=destructive`.

## 2026-06-30T14:26:25+02:00 — DLQ cleanup projection is reusable through MCP; xtask wait/apply races fixed

Loop phase: silo collapse | agent-facing demo surface | tooling reliability
Focus: The DLQ cleanup intelligence was still CLI-private even though agents
should be able to inspect the same read-only projection through MCP. During
verification, two broken tooling steps appeared and were fixed instead of
ignored: concurrent schema bootstrap could hit `tuple concurrently updated`, and
`xtask jobs wait` could transiently report a successful fast job as orphaned
before its `exit_code` file became visible.

Code changes:
- Promoted DLQ triage/cleanup projection DTOs and builders in
  `commands::dlq` to crate-visible helpers.
- Added read-only MCP tools `sinex.dlq_triage` and
  `sinex.dlq_cleanup_plan`, both reusing the CLI projection code and both
  declaring their composed backing RPC reads (`dlq.list`, `dlq.peek`,
  `sources.show`).
- Added a PostgreSQL advisory lock around `sinex_schema::apply::apply`, so all
  schema-apply callers serialize at the database entrypoint rather than relying
  on shell-wrapper-specific locks.
- Added a short settle/recheck loop before a dead background job is synthesized
  as orphaned, plus a regression test for delayed `exit_code` creation.

Verification:
- `nix develop --command xtask check --fmt -p xtask -p sinex-schema -p sinexctl`
  passed.
- Built fixed `schema-apply-bootstrap` and ran two simultaneous applies against
  the checkout-local DB; both exited 0:
  `schema-apply-concurrency-a-20260630T121827Z.log` and
  `schema-apply-concurrency-b-20260630T121827Z.log`.
- Live MCP `tools/list` captured
  `mcp-tools-list-dlq-projections-20260630T121451Z.json`, showing
  `sinex.dlq_triage` and `sinex.dlq_cleanup_plan`.
- Live MCP `tools/call` captured
  `mcp-dlq-cleanup-plan-all-retained-20260630T121502Z.json`, returning 283
  purge-candidate messages, 0 blockers, and 3 coalesced purge ranges.
- Job 2000547 passed: `xtask build -p sinex-schema -p sinexctl`.
- Job 2000548 passed the focused DLQ cleanup projection tests.
- Job 2000549 passed:
  `xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_settled_terminal_status_waits_for_delayed_exit_code) or test(test_terminal_status_from_exit_code_file)'`.

Demo update:
- `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/README.md` now includes MCP
  projection artifacts and the schema-apply concurrency proof.

## 2026-06-30T14:49:10+02:00 — xtask status should not count managed runtime as active work

Loop phase: tooling reliability | observability cleanup
Focus: after launching checkout-local `sinexd` through `xtask run`, the quick
`xtask status --summary` view reported `jobs:1` / `active_jobs: 1` even when no
build, check, or test job was active. The raw jobs registry correctly knew about
the long-lived runtime, and service status needed that row, but the human
summary conflated managed service lifecycle with active development work.

Fix:
- Keep the raw active-job list for service probing.
- Filter status-managed service jobs (`sinexd`) out of the summary active-work
  count and job details.
- Add a regression test so a running `sinexd` job is classified as managed
  service work while a running `xtask` job is not.
- Removed the wrapper path that reused a stale checkout-local xtask binary when
  xtask sources were newer for read-only/no-compile commands.

Verification:
- Committed as `c550405d7 fix(xtask): rebuild stale status binary`.
- `nix develop --command xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(status_managed_service_jobs_are_not_active_work)'`
  passed.
- `nix develop --command xtask check --fmt -p xtask` passed.
- `nix develop --command xtask status --summary` reported `active_jobs: 0` and
  `summary: ... jobs:0 ...` while service probes still reported `sinexd`.

## 2026-06-30T14:55:46+02:00 — background watchdog killed the dev runtime

Loop phase: runtime reliability | live-store freshness
Focus: after the status summary fix, `xtask status --summary` still reported
stale `event_engine` liveness. Live process inspection found no `sinexd`
process, `sinexctl runtime health` returned connection refused, and job 2000536
had exit code 124. Its logs showed `sinexd` receiving SIGTERM at
2026-06-30T12:49:08Z after roughly one hour of runtime, followed by xtask
marking the job killed. The root cause is the generic background-job watchdog:
`JobManager` spawned a detached reaper for every non-test background job, so
`xtask run core --bg` was treated like a bounded command instead of a long-lived
local runtime.

Fix:
- Make background watchdog policy explicit.
- Do not attach a watchdog to `xtask run ... --bg`.
- Also classify the spawned `sinexd` binary path as long-lived runtime work,
  because `xtask run core --bg` records the managed runtime process itself as
  the background job command.
- Preserve bounded watchdogs for `xtask test` and ordinary background commands.
- Restart checkout-local `sinexd` and verify runtime telemetry becomes fresh.

Verification:
- Killed the already-attached stale reaper for old runtime job 2000555 before it
  could terminate the live runtime later.
- `nix develop --command xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_background_watchdog_skips_long_lived_run_jobs)'`
  passed.
- `nix develop --command xtask check --fmt -p xtask` passed.
- Cancelled old runtime job 2000555, relaunched `nix develop --command xtask run
  core --bg`, and got runtime job 2000556.
- `ps -eo ... | rg 'sinexd|__reap|2000556'` showed the `sinexd` process and no
  `__reap` process for job 2000556.
- `nix develop --command xtask status --summary` reported
  `event_engine:ok`, `lag:0`, `active_jobs: 0`, raw `sinexd` background service
  healthy, and no stale telemetry warning.
- `sinexctl --runtime-target .sinex/state/runtime-target.json runtime health
  --insecure --format json` returned `serving: true`; the remaining degradation
  is the known raw-ingest DLQ pressure.

Focus: after stabilizing checkout-local `sinexd`, runtime health stayed

Evidence:

- Repeated list sample still reports `last_seq: 318`; the tail is not currently

- `ops dlq cleanup-plan --all-retained` classifies all 298 retained messages as

- Coalesced cleanup actions are:
  - `1..235` (235 messages, duplicate occurrences + historical material errors)
  - `246..249` (4 slice-arrival timeouts)
  - `260..318` (59 slice-arrival/corruption historical material errors)
- `runtime health` is serving but degraded only by raw-ingest DLQ pressure.

Artifacts:
- `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/dlq-list-20260630T1303Z.json`
- `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/dlq-cleanup-plan-all-retained-20260630T1303Z.json`
- `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/dlq-triage-all-retained-20260630T1303Z.json`
- `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/runtime-health-dlq-pressure-20260630T1303Z.json`

Decision:
- Do not purge without explicit operator confirmation.
- Improve the read-only DLQ pressure recommendation so `dlq.list` points at the
  richer cleanup-plan projection (`ops dlq cleanup-plan --all-retained`) rather

## 2026-06-30T15:16:57+02:00 — xtask pre-suite failures and wrapper stage temp files

Loop phase: tooling reliability | verification-path repair
Focus: the first focused DLQ test invocation failed before test execution, but
xtask reported it as `No tests discovered`. The actual stderr tail contained a
pre-suite cargo/nextest command failure. A concurrent wrapper invocation also
printed `mv: cannot stat .../xtask-build.lock/stages.json.tmp`, showing the
devshell wrapper's stage-metrics temp file was not concurrency-safe.

Fix:
- `xtask/src/nextest/reporter.rs` now classifies pre-suite `error: command ... exited`
  stderr as `nextest failed before test discovery` while preserving the old
  zero-discovery message for true no-suite cases.
- Added regression coverage for the live failure shape.
- `flake.nix` wrapper stage metrics now use a unique temp file
  (`stages.json.$pid.$nanos.tmp`) and atomically rename for both existing and
  initial metrics writes.

Verification:
- `nix develop --command xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(no_suite_started_command_failure_is_not_reported_as_zero_tests)'`
  passed.
- `nix develop --command xtask check --fmt -p xtask` passed.
- `nix develop --command xtask check --fmt -p sinexd` passed.
- Re-entering through `nix develop` after the `flake.nix` edit rebuilt the
  wrapper path without repeating the shared `stages.json.tmp` `mv` failure.

Blocked proof:
- `nix develop --command xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(dlq_pressure_contracts) or test(dlq_pressure_signal_carries_runtime_action_and_batch_limit)'`
  is currently blocked by host pressure: earlyoom killed `rustc`, then `xtask`,
  at 2026-06-30T15:15:19+02:00.
- A one-shot lower-memory retry with `CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1` and mold
  `--threads=1` was counterproductive because `RUSTFLAGS` invalidated build
  artifacts and earlyoom killed a fresh proc-macro link.

Decision:
- Do not hide this as a test failure; it is a live host resource failure.
- Do not turn it into permanent cargo/build policy.
- Keep the DLQ recommendation source edit separate from fully verified runtime
  proof until the host has enough headroom to link the sinexd test/runtime binary.

## 2026-06-30T16:13:14+02:00 — earlyoom threshold bug caused swap-only kill loop

Loop phase: host reliability | devloop velocity protection
Focus: the sinexd test-link failures were not ordinary test failures. Live
journal evidence showed earlyoom repeatedly killing `ld.mold`, `rustc`,
`xtask`, checkout-local Postgres children, `nats-server`, `weechat`, and
`Xwayland` while `/proc/meminfo` still reported roughly 13-21 GiB
`MemAvailable`.

Evidence:
- earlyoom command line was `-m95 -s20`.
- earlyoom logs at 2026-06-30T15:18+02:00 showed `mem avail` around 50% but
  still "at or below SIGTERM limits: mem 95.00%, swap 20.00%".
- During `swapoff`, swap total temporarily reached 0; earlyoom treated
  `swap free: 0 of 0` as below the swap threshold and killed `Xwayland` despite
  85% available RAM.
- The dev-local Postgres checkpointer was selected repeatedly because it had
  `oom_score_adj=200`; it did not exit from SIGTERM, causing a repeated kill
  attempt loop.

Fix in progress:
- Stopped `earlyoom.service` immediately to end the kill loop.
- Re-enabled `/swap/swapfile`, clearing swap used to 0.
- Patched `/realm/project/sinnix/modules/performance.nix` so earlyoom requires
  genuinely low RAM (`freeMemThreshold = 20`) as well as low swap
  (`freeSwapThreshold = 20`), and expanded the avoid regex to match the local
  dev infra/desktop processes the comment already claimed to protect
  (`postgres`, `nats-server`, `Xwayland`, `weechat`).
- Running `nix develop --command switch` in `/realm/project/sinnix` to apply
  the fix through the configured system path.

Decision:
- This is a Sinnix runtime-policy bug, not a Sinex compile policy bug.
- Do not lower Sinex build/test defaults to work around it.
- Do not retry heavy Sinex linking until the live earlyoom command line is
  verified sane.

## 2026-06-30T16:21:36+02:00 — earlyoom fixed, runtime restored, DLQ recommendation live

Loop phase: live runtime hygiene | demo artifact closure
Focus: after applying the Sinnix earlyoom fix, recover the checkout-local Sinex
runtime and complete the DLQ pressure recommendation slice.

Host/runtime verification:
- Sinnix commit `57b6309 fix(perf): require real RAM pressure for earlyoom`
  landed in `/realm/project/sinnix`.
- `nix develop --command switch` applied it live.
- `systemctl status earlyoom` shows `-m20 -s20`, avoid regex includes
  `Xwayland`, `weechat`, `postgres`, and `nats-server`.
- `/proc/swaps` shows `/swap/swapfile` active with 0 used.
- `journalctl -u earlyoom --since 2026-06-30T16:15:38+02:00` shows startup
  only and no new kill events.

Sinex verification:
- `xtask infra start` recovered checkout-local Postgres/NATS after the earlier
  earlyoom kills.
- `xtask run core --bg` rebuilt and launched `sinexd` as job 2000557.
- `sinexctl runtime health` is serving with healthy database and NATS; the only
  degradation is the expected raw-ingest DLQ pressure.
- `xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(dlq_pressure_signal_carries_runtime_action_and_batch_limit)'`
  passed after the earlyoom fix.
- `xtask check --fmt -p sinexd` passed.
- Live `sinexctl ops dlq list` now reports
  `recommended_action: "ops dlq cleanup-plan --all-retained"` and captured
  `/realm/inbox/demos_sinex/dlq-pressure-live/dlq-list-cleanup-plan-recommendation-20260630T1421Z.json`.

Decision:
- Commit the DLQ recommendation as a small Sinex fix.
- Leave destructive DLQ purge unrun until explicit operator confirmation.

Follow-up:
- A newer Sinnix working-tree refinement appeared after the first commit:
  `vm.swappiness = 0`, `freeMemThreshold = 7`, `freeSwapThreshold = 75`, a
  broader earlyoom avoid regex, and `earlyoom.after/wants = swap.target`.
- This better matches the intended policy: avoid routine swapping, use the
  4 GiB swapfile as a tripwire once roughly 1 GiB is occupied, and ensure
  earlyoom does not start while the swap device is absent during activation.
- Applying that refinement with a second `nix develop --command switch` before
  retrying broad Sinex verification.

## 2026-06-30T16:26:14+02:00 — final host/runtime status after earlyoom refinement

Loop phase: broken-step closure | live runtime proof | next-slice selection
Focus: close the "do not step over broken steps" loop with concrete evidence
after the refined Sinnix policy and Sinex DLQ recommendation commits.

Evidence:
- Sinnix commit `71048a3 fix(perf): treat swap as pressure tripwire` is live.
- `systemctl status earlyoom` now shows
  `/bin/earlyoom -m7 -n -r3600 -s75 ...`.
- earlyoom startup log says SIGTERM only when memory available is <= 7% and
  swap free is <= 75%; current state is `mem avail: 20706 of 25314 MiB
  (81.80%), swap free: 4095 of 4095 MiB (100.00%)`.
- `free -h` reports 31 GiB total, 10 GiB used, 20 GiB available, 0 swap used.
- `/proc/swaps` reports the intended 4 GiB `/swap/swapfile`; no zram swap is
  active.
- `xtask status --summary` reports checkout-local Postgres/NATS healthy,
  `sinexd` job 2000557 running as PID 1621474, event-engine healthy, consumer
  lag 0, and git clean.
- `sinexctl runtime health` is serving with healthy DB/NATS; the sole
  degradation remains raw-ingest DLQ pressure:
  303 pending message(s), sequence span 323.

Conclusion:
- The immediate host-kill brokenness is fixed in Sinnix, not worked around in
  Sinex.
- The "this xtask version" broken step is also fixed: stale status-binary
  rebuild behavior is committed as `c550405d7`, run-job liveness as `547fc26b0`,
  and pre-suite failures no longer masquerade as zero-test discovery as
  `fc88887bb`.
- Next object-level Sinex work should either turn the retained DLQ cleanup-plan
  evidence into a non-destructive demo/API surface, or continue a query/context
  slice. Do not purge the retained DLQ without explicit operator confirmation.

## 2026-06-30T16:33:20+02:00 — fixed remaining xtask status/lock observability break

Loop phase: broken-step closure | tooling reliability
Focus: the recovery proof exposed two remaining xtask issues:
`xtask jobs active` showed the long-lived `sinexd` job, but
`xtask status --summary` said `jobs:0`; additionally every xtask command kept
printing `Removing stale xtask build lock with no pid file`.

Findings:
- The `jobs:0` count was intentionally filtering status-managed service jobs
  out of "active work" so a normal `xtask run core --bg` would not make the
  developer work queue look busy forever. The problem was naming: compact
  status and JSON called that filtered count `jobs` / `active_jobs`, which
  conflicts with `xtask jobs active`.
- The stale lock directory contained only `stages.json`. Root cause:
  `_sinex_xtask_stage_record` stored metrics under `xtask-build.lock/` and
  always created the parent directory. SQLx bootstrap can call the same helper
  outside an owned rebuild lock, recreating a pidless lock directory after a
  successful cleanup.

Changes:
- Status summary now reports `work:<n> services:<n>` instead of ambiguous
  `jobs:<n>`.
- Summary JSON now exposes `active_work_jobs` and `active_service_jobs` and no
  longer serializes the ambiguous `active_jobs` field.
- Wrapper stage recording now no-ops unless the current wrapper process owns
  `xtask-build.lock/pid`, so read-only/status commands cannot recreate a stale
  pidless build-lock directory.

Verification:
- `nix develop --command xtask check --fmt -p xtask` passed.
- `nix develop --command xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(status_managed_service_jobs_are_not_active_work)|test(test_summary_output_json_shape)'`
  ran 2 tests and passed.
- Fresh-wrapper proof:
  `nix develop --command bash -lc 'rm -rf .sinex/state/xtask-build.lock; xtask status --summary --json >/tmp/sinex-status-wrapper-lock-proof.json; test ! -d .sinex/state/xtask-build.lock; jq -r .data.summary /tmp/sinex-status-wrapper-lock-proof.json'`
  passed and printed
  `infra:ok work:0 services:1 tests:ok warns:123w fixes:15f event_engine:ok lag:24 batch:- git:dirty bypass:20`.

## 2026-06-30T16:45:34+02:00 — promoted DLQ cleanup-plan to shared view

Loop phase: silo collapse | operator evidence projection | live demo proof
Focus: the DLQ cleanup-plan JSON/table was useful but still owned by
`sinexctl` private structs. That creates a projection silo: demos, API, TUI, and
MCP would need to copy the same retained-backlog evidence shape later.

Changes:
- Added `DlqCleanupPlanView`, `DlqCleanupActionView`, and
  `DlqCleanupPlanItemView` to `sinex_primitives::views::operations`.
- Added schema version `sinex.dlq-cleanup-plan/v1`.
- Moved `sinexctl ops dlq cleanup-plan` construction/rendering onto the shared
  view types; the command remains read-only.
- Added `JsonSchema` derives to the shared `RuntimePressureLevel` and
  `RuntimePressureAction` wire enums so operation/DLQ view schemas can include
  typed pressure fields.

Verification:
- First focused run correctly failed because `RuntimePressureLevel` lacked
  `JsonSchema`; fixed that rather than weakening the view contract.

- Fixed `xtask --version` provenance output before continuing the next query

    presenting the sentinel as a real build time.

    `build: reproducible/unknown (SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH=315532800)` for the
    sentinel while preserving real build timestamps.
  - Process note: I accidentally launched fmt and focused test foreground
    checks in parallel against the same checkout. They completed cleanly, but
    this is still a workflow miss; serialize foreground `xtask` checks unless
    they are intentionally background-managed by `xtask jobs`.
